Summary: This study compares handcrafted radiomics (HCR) and deep learning radiomics (DLR) models, with and without clinical factors, for predicting the response to concurrent chemoradiotherapy (CRT) in patients with locally advanced cervical cancer (LACC). The study found that both HCR and DLR models could predict CRT responses, with DLR models generally outperforming HCR models. The integration of clinical factors tended to improve performance in HCR analysis. The authors conclude that their findings may contribute to the development of personalized treatment strategies for LACC patients.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to any specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines.

Item2.reason: The study defines eligibility criteria for patient inclusion, aiming for a representative study population. The inclusion and exclusion criteria are clearly stated in the Methods section under 'Study population'.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is based on pelvic MRI and biopsy using RECIST criteria version 1.1, assessed 3 months after CRT, which is a reasonable standard for evaluating treatment response.

Item4.reason: The study explicitly states that the data comes from a single institution.

Item5.reason: The imaging data source is contrast-enhanced T1-weighted and T2-weighted MRI, which are standard sequences in cervical cancer imaging. However, there is no mention of specific acquisition protocol guidelines.

Item6.reason: The study mentions the use of contrast-enhanced T1-weighted and T2-weighted MRI. More detailed information about image acquisition process was described in Supplementary material.

Item7.reason: The response evaluation was performed 3 months after CRT, which is an appropriate interval to assess treatment response.

Condition1.reason: The study involves segmentation of the primary tumor on MRI images for handcrafted radiomics analysis.

Condition2.reason: The segmentation was performed semi-manually by two radiation oncologists, so it is not fully automated.

Item8.reason: The study mentions that the primary tumor was semi-manually segmented by two radiation oncologists using the Eclipse treatment planning system.

Item9.reason: The segmentation was not fully automated, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: The segmentation in the test set was performed by two readers, not a single reader or automated tool.

Condition3.reason: The study uses handcrafted radiomic features.

Item11.reason: The study does not provide detailed information about image preprocessing techniques, such as gray level normalization or image registration. The description of image preprocessing is not transparent.

Item12.reason: The study does not explicitly mention the use of standardized feature extraction software compliant with IBSI. The Eclipse treatment planning system was used for segmentation, but it is unclear if it was also used for feature extraction.

Item13.reason: The study mentions the extraction of 1890 imaging features for HCR analysis and describes the use of a 3D convolutional neural network for DLR analysis. The neural network architecture is described.

Condition4.reason: The study includes tabular data in the form of radiomic features and clinical factors.

Condition5.reason: The study uses a 3D convolutional neural network for end-to-end deep learning.

Item14.reason: The study does not mention the removal of non-robust features through test-retest, reproducibility, or stability analysis.

Item15.reason: The study applies logistic regression and recursive feature elimination to select 20 imaging features for the HCR analysis.

Item16.reason: The study does not provide a clear justification for the appropriateness of the dimensionality compared to the data size.

Item17.reason: The study does not include a robustness assessment of the end-to-end deep learning pipeline in a test-retest setting or with image perturbations.

Item18.reason: The study mentions that the dataset was randomly divided into training and test datasets using the 'createDataPartition' function in R. However, it does not explicitly state whether the data split was stratified or if it was done on the patient level.

Item19.reason: The study does not explicitly address the handling of potential confounding factors, such as scanner type or variations in imaging protocols.

Item20.reason: The study reports AUC, balanced accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, negative predictive value, and F1-score for classification tasks. Confusion matrices are also presented.

Item21.reason: The study includes 95% confidence intervals for the AUC values.

Item22.reason: The study does not explicitly mention calibration assessment of the final models.

Item23.reason: The study uses both T1-weighted and T2-weighted MRI without a comparison to uni-parametric imaging.

Item24.reason: The study does not compare the radiomic approach with a non-radiomic method representative of clinical practice. There is no proof of added clinical value.

Item25.reason: The study does not compare the models with a simple baseline reference model.

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an independent data set that is sampled from the same source as the training set.

Item27.reason: The study does not include external testing on data from other institutions.

Item28.reason: The study states that the datasets are not publicly available due to privacy protection policies.

Item29.reason: The code is made available open-source along with our experimental results at https://github.com/youhs4554/radiomics_CRT.

Item30.reason: The study does not explicitly state whether the final model is shared in the form of a raw model file or as a ready-to-use system.
